echo "=== Sections ==="
readelf -S "$DBGFILE" 2>/dev/null | head -40

echo ""
echo "=== Symbol table counts ==="
readelf -s --wide "$DBGFILE" 2>/dev/null | head -5
readelf -s --wide "$DBGFILE" 2>/dev/null | wc -l

echo ""
echo "=== Any FUNC symbol near these addresses ==="

    addr = strtonum("0x" $2)
    if (addr >= 0x2f0000 && addr <= 0x300000) print
  }
}' | head -20

arguments.command:
SOFILE="/tmp/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so"

# Check section layout in the actual binary to confirm where the SGen addresses fall
echo "=== Section layout (actual binary) ==="
readelf -S --wide "$SOFILE" 2>/dev/null | grep -E '^\s+\[' | awk '{printf "%-30s addr=%-18s size=%-14s flags=%s\n", $2, $4, $6, $8}'

echo ""
echo "=== What section contains 0x2f4b20? ==="
readelf -S --wide "$SOFILE" 2>/dev/null | awk '
  /^\s+\[/ {
    name=$2; addr=strtonum("0x" $4); size=strtonum("0x" $6); flags=$8
    end = addr + size
    if (addr <= 0x2f4b20 && end > 0x2f4b20) printf "0x2f4b20 is in section %s (0x%x - 0x%x) flags=%s\n", name, addr, end, flags
    if (addr <= 0x2f4c84 && end > 0x2f4c84) printf "0x2f4c84 is in section %s (0x%x - 0x%x) flags=%s\n", name, addr, end, flags
  }'

data.reasoningText:
Now I see the issue. The `.text` section starts at `0x000c2480` and has size `0x22a078`, so it ends at approximately `0x2ec4f8`. The addresses `0x2f4b20` and `0x2f4c84` are in section `.data.rel.ro` (starts at `0x2f3810`), not in `.text`. This means those addresses are NOT code addresses.

Wait, but this is a tombstone backtrace - those addresses are program counter values. In Android tombstones, the `pc` values are relative to the load address of the library (they're virtual addresses within the ELF). Let me re-check...


Actually, looking at the section layout more carefully, I can see that `.text` ends around `0x2ec4f8`, then `.plt` follows, and then `.data.rel.ro` starts at `0x2f3810`. The addresses `0x2f4b20` and `0x2f4c84` fall within `.data.rel.ro`, which means they're pointing to data, not code.

This is suspicious for PC values in a backtrace—they should be pointing to executable code. Either the stack is corrupted, the unwinder got confused, or there's something unusual happening with how these frames were captured. Let me verify the offset: `0x2f4b20` is about `0x1310` bytes into `.data.rel.ro`, which definitely isn't code. The SGen worker thread frames might be artifacts of stack corruption or incorrect unwinding rather than genuine call frames.

I need to check whether `.data.rel.ro` actually has execute permissions. Looking at the section flags, it only has `WA` (Write, Alloc)—no Execute flag. So these addresses are definitely in a non-executable section, which confirms they shouldn't be valid PC values. The debug file sections should mirror the original binary's layout, so I need to verify the actual section mapping in the binary itself.

The `$d.147` symbol is an ARM data mapping symbol, not a function, which explains why the unwinder got confused. The interp.c reference is probably just the nearest compilation unit. Let me try using `objdump` or `llvm-addr2line` to see if there's any actual function nearby, or check the `.text` section boundaries more carefully to understand what's happening.

Looking at the section layout, `.text` ends at `0x2ec4f8`, but the SGen worker addresses `0x2f4b20` and `0x2f4c84` are actually beyond that—they're in the space between `.plt` and `.data.rel.ro`. This suggests the unwinder might have jumped into a completely different section or there's a gap I'm not accounting for. addresses). I should note in the symbolication result that these are data pointers the unwinder mistook for return addresses. Let me double-check by examining the original binary's section headers alongside the debug file to make sure the virtual address mappings are accurate.

    if (addr <= 0x2f4b20 && end > 0x2f4b20) printf "0x2f4b20 is in section %s (0x%x - 0x%x) flags=%s\n", name, addr, end, flags
    if (addr <= 0x2f4c84 && end > 0x2f4c84) printf "0x2f4c84 is in section %s (0x%x - 0x%x) flags=%s\n", name, addr, end, flags
  }'
